policy_statement: Raise Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS) for household appliances and introduce MEPS for more appliances.
mechanism_description: Raise and introduce minimum energy performance standards for household appliances to increase appliance efficiency and reduce household energy use and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (appliance function) → ↓ energy input (kWh or fuel) → ↓ fuel combustion for power/heat → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Households and retailers shift toward higher-efficiency appliances due to set minimum performance set (and associated product eligibility rules). (2) Mediating process: Higher appliance efficiency reduces electricity/fuel consumed per unit of service delivered; this lowers aggregate load on the grid and/or onsite fuel use. (3) Outcome: Reduced energy demand leads to reduced CO₂ (and other GHG) emissions from electricity generation and direct combustion, with potential co-benefits like lower peak demand.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational energy demand from appliances, leading to lower upstream electricity/heat generation emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: efficiency standards → same household service with less energy → reduced energy supply combustion → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The mechanism works by reducing energy input per service (efficiency/resource productivity), not by directly deploying low-carbon generation (Mitigation) or reducing climate hazard exposure (Adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is already fully decarbonized, emissions benefits shrink, but the dominant effect remains energy-use reduction (resource efficiency). Rebound effects may offset some savings but do not change the primary pathway.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that raises/introduces appliance MEPS is fundamentally an energy-efficiency intervention that reduces operational energy demand for the same services.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the net benefit is mainly CO₂ reduction, peak-load reduction, or fuel-switching depends on grid mix, appliance types, and enforcement—handled at policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on grid carbon intensity and enforcement; potential rebound (more/larger appliance use) can reduce realized savings; some MEPS may also reduce peak demand (system benefits) but the primary mechanism remains energy-efficiency-driven demand reduction.
key_indicators: "Raise Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS)"; "introduce MEPS for more appliances"
co_benefits: Lower household energy bills; reduced peak demand and improved grid reliability; improved air quality from reduced fossil generation
